title: Realms: Isolated Identity Tenants
description:
## A Realm Is a Self-Contained Security Domain

A **realm** in Keycloak is an **isolated tenant**. Each realm has its **own** users, clients, roles, groups, identity providers, authentication flows, and **its own cryptographic signing keys**. Users and configuration in one realm are completely invisible to another realm — a user in `acme` cannot log into a client registered in `globex`.

### The special `master` realm

When Keycloak first starts it creates a **`master` realm**. This realm exists to **administer Keycloak itself** — the initial admin account lives here, and it can manage all other realms.

> **Best practice:** Treat `master` as the *operators-only* realm. Do **not** put your application's end users or app clients in `master`. Instead, create a **dedicated application realm** (e.g. `acme-app`) for each application or tenant. This isolates blast radius, lets you use separate keys and login policies, and keeps administrative access cleanly separated from end-user access.

### Why one realm per app / tenant?

- **Isolation** — a misconfiguration or breach in one realm doesn't expose another.
- **Independent policy** — different password rules, token lifespans, MFA requirements, and themes per realm.
- **Independent keys** — each realm signs its own tokens, so trust boundaries are clean.
- **Independent identity providers** — one tenant can federate with Google, another with a corporate SAML IdP.

### What lives inside a realm

| Realm-scoped object | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Users** | Accounts that authenticate in this realm |
| **Clients** | Applications/services registered to use this realm |
| **Realm roles** | Permissions defined at the realm level |
| **Client roles** | Permissions scoped to a specific client |
| **Groups** | Collections of users with shared roles/attributes |
| **Identity providers** | External login sources (Google, GitHub, SAML/OIDC IdPs) |
| **Client scopes & mappers** | Reusable token claim configuration |
| **Authentication flows** | The configurable login/registration sequences |
| **Keys** | The realm's signing/encryption key pairs |
| **Realm settings** | Token lifespans, login policies, security defenses, themes |

> **Mental model:** A realm is like a separate apartment building. The `master` realm is the building manager's office; your tenants each get their own building with their own keys, residents, and house rules.
